questions.question: The joint PMF of two discrete random variables X and Y is given by pXY(x,y) = kxy for x = 1,2,3 and y = 1,2,3. What is the value of the constant k?
The joint PMF of two discrete random variables X and Y is given by pXY(x,y) = (1/36)xy for x = 1,2,3 and y = 1,2,3. What is the marginal PMF of X?
The joint PMF of two discrete random variables X and Y is given by pXY(x,y) = (1/36)xy for x = 1,2,3 and y = 1,2,3. What is the probability P(1 ≤ X ≤ 2, Y ≤ 2)?
A fair coin is tossed three times. Let X be the number of heads in the first two tosses, and let Y be the number of heads in the third toss. What is the joint PMF pXY(1,1)?
Two random variables X and Y have the joint PMF: pXY(1,1)=0.10, pXY(2,2)=0.35, pXY(3,3)=0.05, and pXY(4,4)=0.50. What is the value of the joint CDF FXY(2,2)?
Two random variables X and Y have the joint PMF: pXY(1,1)=0.10, pXY(2,2)=0.35, pXY(3,3)=0.05, and pXY(4,4)=0.50. What is P(1 ≤ X ≤ 2, Y ≤ 2)?
Two random variables X and Y have the joint PMF: pXY(1,1)=0.10, pXY(2,2)=0.35, pXY(3,3)=0.05, and pXY(4,4)=0.50. What is the value of the joint CDF FXY(1,2)?(من عندي)
Two discrete random variables X and Y have the joint CDF values: FXY(0,0)=1/12, FXY(0,1)=1/3, FXY(0,2)=2/3, FXY(1,0)=1/6, FXY(1,1)=7/12, and FXY(1,2)=1. What is P(0 < X < 2, 0 < Y < 2)?
Two discrete random variables X and Y have the joint CDF values: FXY(0,0)=1/12, FXY(0,1)=1/3, FXY(0,2)=2/3, FXY(1,0)=1/6, FXY(1,1)=7/12, and FXY(1,2)=1. What is the marginal CDF FX(1)?
Two discrete random variables X and Y have the joint CDF values: FXY(0,0)=1/12, FXY(0,1)=1/3, FXY(0,2)=2/3, FXY(1,0)=1/6, FXY(1,1)=7/12, and FXY(1,2)=1. What is P(X=1,Y=1)?

Two discrete random variables X and Y have the joint PMF: p(1,1)=1/12, p(1,2)=1/6, p(1,3)=1/12, p(2,1)=1/6, p(2,2)=1/4, p(2,3)=1/12, p(3,1)=1/12, and all other values are 0. What is the marginal PMF pX(1)?
Two discrete random variables X and Y have the joint PMF: p(1,1)=1/12, p(1,2)=1/6, p(1,3)=1/12, p(2,1)=1/6, p(2,2)=1/4, p(2,3)=1/12, p(3,1)=1/12, and all other values are 0. What is P(X < 2.5)?

Two discrete random variables X and Y have the joint PMF: p(1,1)=1/12, p(1,2)=1/6, p(1,3)=1/12, p(2,1)=1/6, p(2,2)=1/4, p(2,3)=1/12, p(3,1)=1/12, and all other values are 0. What is the probability that Y is odd?
Two discrete random variables X and Y have the joint PMF: p(1,1)=0.2, p(1,2)=0.1, p(2,1)=0.1, p(2,2)=0.2, p(3,1)=0.1, and p(3,2)=0.3. What is the marginal PMF pX(2)?
Two discrete random variables X and Y have the joint PMF: p(1,1)=0.2, p(1,2)=0.1, p(2,1)=0.1, p(2,2)=0.2, p(3,1)=0.1, and p(3,2)=0.3. What is the conditional probability pX|Y(1|2)?
Two discrete random variables X and Y have the joint PMF: p(1,1)=0.2, p(1,2)=0.1, p(2,1)=0.1, p(2,2)=0.2, p(3,1)=0.1, and p(3,2)=0.3. Are X and Y independent?
options: Yes, because pXY(x,y)=pX(x)pY(y) for all x and y
No, because pXY(x,y)≠pX(x)pY(y) for all x and y
No, because X and Y have different ranges
questions.answer: No, because pXY(x,y)≠pX(x)pY(y) for all x and y

questions.question: The joint PDF of two continuous random variables X and Y is fXY(x,y) = kx for 0 < y ≤ x < 1 and 0 otherwise. What is the value of k?
The joint PDF of two continuous random variables X and Y is fXY(x,y) = 3x for 0 < y ≤ x < 1. What is the marginal PDF fX(x)?
The joint CDF of two continuous random variables is FXY(x,y) = 1 − e^(−ax)e^(−by) + e^(−(ax+by)) for x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0. What is the marginal PDF of X?
The joint CDF is FXY(x,y) = 1 − e^(−ax)e^(−by). What is the marginal PDF of Y?
Two continuous random variables X and Y have the joint PDF fXY(x,y) = k e^{-(2x+3y)} for x ≥ 0 and y ≥ 0. What value of k makes fXY(x,y) a valid joint PDF?
Given the joint PDF fXY(x,y) = 6 e^{-(2x+3y)} for x ≥ 0 and y ≥ 0, what is the marginal PDF of X?
Given the joint PDF fXY(x,y) = 6 e^{-(2x+3y)} for x ≥ 0 and y ≥ 0, what is the marginal PDF of Y?
Given the joint PDF fXY(x,y) = 6 e^{-(2x+3y)} for x ≥ 0 and y ≥ 0, what is P(X < 1, Y < 0.5)?
Two continuous random variables X and Y have the joint PDF fXY(x,y) = k(1 − x^2y) for 0 ≤ x ≤ 1 and 0 ≤ y ≤ 1. What value of k makes fXY(x,y) a valid joint PDF?
Given fXY(x,y) = (6/5)(1 − x^2y) for 0 ≤ x ≤ 1 and 0 ≤ y ≤ 1, what is the conditional PDF fX|Y(x|y)?
Given fXY(x,y) = (6/5)(1 − x^2y) for 0 ≤ x ≤ 1 and 0 ≤ y ≤ 1, what is the conditional PDF fY|X(y|x)?
Two continuous random variables X and Y have joint PDF fXY(x,y) = (6/7)(x^2 + xy/2) for 0 < x < 1 and 0 < y < 2. What is the CDF FX(x) of X for 0 < x < 1?
Given the joint PDF fXY(x,y) = (6/7)(x^2 + xy/2) for 0 < x < 1 and 0 < y < 2, what is P(X > Y)?
Given the joint PDF fXY(x,y) = (6/7)(x^2 + xy/2) for 0 < x < 1 and 0 < y < 2, what is P(Y > 1/2 | X < 1/2)?
Two continuous random variables X and Y have the joint PDF fXY(x,y) = k e^{-(x+y)} for x ≥ 0 and y ≥ x. What value of k makes fXY(x,y) a valid joint PDF?
Given the joint PDF fXY(x,y) = 2e^{-(x+y)} for x ≥ 0 and y ≥ x, what is P(Y < 2X)?
The joint PDF is fXY(x,y) = (6x/7) on the region 1 ≤ x + y ≤ 2, x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0. Which integral correctly represents P(Y > X/2)?(not in the exam 85%)
The joint PDF is fXY(x,y) = (6x/7) on the region 1 ≤ x + y ≤ 2, x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0. What is the exact value of P(X > Y)?
The joint PDF is fXY(x,y) = (6x/7) on the region 1 ≤ x + y ≤ 2, x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0. What is the exact value of P(X > Y)?
Two continuous random variables X and Y have the joint PDF fXY(x,y) = (1/2)e^{-2y} for 0 ≤ x ≤ 4 and y ≥ 0. What is the marginal PDF fX(x)?

A box contains 3 red balls and 2 green balls. A ball is selected, observed, replaced, and then another ball is selected. Let X=1 if the first ball is red and X=0 if green. Let Y=1 if the second ball is red and Y=0 if green. What is P(X=1,Y=1)?
A box contains 3 red balls and 2 green balls with replacement after each draw. What is the joint PMF pXY(x,y)?
A box contains 3 red balls and 2 green balls with replacement after each draw, what is the conditional PMF P(X=1 | Y=1)?
Given the joint PDF fXY(x,y)=2e^{-(x+2y)} for x≥0 and y≥0, what is E[X|Y=y]?
Given the joint PDF fXY(x,y)=2e^{-(x+2y)} for x≥0 and y≥0, what is E[Y|X=x]?
A fair coin is tossed four times. Let X be the number of heads in the first two tosses and Y be the number of heads in the last two tosses. What is P(X=1,Y=1)?
A fair coin is tossed four times. Let X be the number of heads in the first two tosses and Y be the number of heads in the last two tosses,Why are X and Y independent in the four-coin experiment?
options: Because X and Y count heads from different tosses
questions.answer: Because X and Y count heads from different tosses

questions.question: Given the joint PDF fXY(x,y)=xye^{-y^2/4} for 0≤x≤1 and y≥0, what is the marginal PDF fX(x)?
Given the joint PDF fXY(x,y)=xye^{-y^2/4} for 0≤x≤1 and y≥0, what is the marginal PDF fY(y)?
Given the joint PDF fXY(x,y)=xye^{-y^2/4} for 0≤x≤1 and y≥0, Are X and Y independent?
options: No, because the PDF contains x and y together

questions.question: A joint PDF is fXY(x,y) = (6/7)x for 1 ≤ x + y ≤ 2, x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0. What is the marginal PDF fX(x)?
A joint PDF is fXY(x,y) = (6/7)x for 1 ≤ x + y ≤ 2, x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0. What is the marginal PDF fX(x)?(not coming 95%)

Two discrete random variables X and Y have the joint PMF given by: P(X=-1,Y=0)=0, P(X=-1,Y=1)=1/3, P(X=0,Y=0)=1/3, P(X=0,Y=1)=0, P(X=1,Y=0)=0, P(X=1,Y=1)=1/3. Are X and Y independent, and what is Cov(X,Y)?
Two events A and B satisfy P(A)=1/4, P(B|A)=1/2, and P(A|B)=1/4. Define X=1 if A occurs and 0 otherwise, and Y=1 if B occurs and 0 otherwise. What are E[X], Var(X), E[Y], and Var(Y)?
Given P(A)=1/4, P(B|A)=1/2, P(A|B)=1/4, define X=1 if A occurs and 0 otherwise, and Y=1 if B occurs and 0 otherwise. What is the correlation ρXY and are X and Y uncorrelated?
options: ρXY = 1/2 and X,Y are not uncorrelated
questions.answer: ρXY = 1/3 and X,Y are not uncorrelated

questions.question: A fair die is tossed three times. Let X be the number of 1's obtained and Y be the number of 3's obtained. What is the correlation coefficient ρXY?

A box contains 10 chips from A, 16 chips from B, and 14 chips from C. A chip is drawn 20 times with replacement. What is the probability that A is drawn exactly 5 times and C is drawn exactly 6 times?
A box contains 10 chips from A, 16 chips from B, and 14 chips from C. A chip is drawn 20 times with replacement. What is the probability that a chip from vendor B is drawn exactly 9 times?

A college professor rating system has probabilities: Excellent = 0.2, Good = 0.5, Fair = 0.2, Bad = 0.1. If 12 professors are randomly selected, what is the probability that exactly 6 are Excellent, 4 are Good, 1 is Fair, and 1 is Bad?

A college professor rating system has probabilities: Excellent = 0.2, Good = 0.5, Fair = 0.2, Bad = 0.1. If 12 professors are randomly selected, what is the probability that exactly 6 are Excellent, 4 are Good, and 2 are Fair?

A college professor rating system has probabilities: Excellent = 0.2, Good = 0.5, Fair = 0.2, Bad = 0.1. If 12 professors are randomly selected, what is the probability that exactly 6 are Excellent and 6 are Good?

A college professor rating system has probabilities: Excellent = 0.2, Good = 0.5, Fair = 0.2, Bad = 0.1. If 12 professors are randomly selected, what is the probability that exactly 4 are Excellent and 3 are Good?
A college professor rating system has probabilities: Excellent = 0.2, Good = 0.5, Fair = 0.2, Bad = 0.1. If 12 professors are randomly selected, what is the probability that exactly 4 are Bad?
A college professor rating system has probabilities: Excellent = 0.2, Good = 0.5, Fair = 0.2, Bad = 0.1. If 12 professors are randomly selected, what is the probability that none are Bad?

Studies on the toasters made by a company indicate that 50% are good, 35% are fair, 10% burn the toast, and 5% catch fire. If a store has 40 of these toasters in stock, what is the probability that exactly 30 are good, 5 are fair, 3 burn the toast, and 2 catch fire?

Studies on the toasters made by a company indicate that 50% are good, 35% are fair, 10% burn the toast, and 5% catch fire. If a store has 40 of these toasters in stock, what is the probability that exactly 30 are good and 4 are fair?

Studies on the toasters made by a company indicate that 50% are good, 35% are fair, 10% burn the toast, and 5% catch fire. If a store has 40 of these toasters in stock, what is the probability that none catches fire?

Studies on the toasters made by a company indicate that 50% are good, 35% are fair, 10% burn the toast, and 5% catch fire. If a store has 40 of these toasters in stock, what is the probability that none burns the toast and none catches fire?

Ten pieces of candy are given out at random to a group that consists of 8 boys, 7 girls, and 5 adults. If anyone can get more than one piece of candy, what is the probability that exactly 4 pieces go to the girls and 2 pieces go to the adults?

Ten pieces of candy are given out at random to a group that consists of 8 boys, 7 girls, and 5 adults. If anyone can get more than one piece of candy, what is the probability that exactly 5 pieces go to the boys?
